title: Japanese Yaki Udon

ingredients.name: udon noodles (fresh, frozen or dried)
visual_checks: dried udon must be boiled first; fresh or frozen go straight into the wok
ingredients.notes: dried udon must be boiled first; fresh or frozen go straight into the wok

subs.name: thick Chinese hakka noodles (boiled) or even thick cooked wheat vermicelli (semiya)
subs.notes: closest texture widely available in India

subs.name: thinly sliced capsicum or broccoli florets for variety
2 tbsp coconut aminos + 1 tsp salt for a soy-free version

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
visual_checks: added at the end for fragrance
ingredients.notes: added at the end for fragrance

subs.name: omit or use a tiny drop of mustard oil for a pungent Indian twist — it changes the flavour significantly
subs.notes: toasted sesame oil is the authentic choice

steps.text: If using dried udon, bring a large pot of water to a boil and cook the noodles until just tender, about 8–10 minutes. Drain and rinse under cold water to stop cooking. Fresh or frozen udon just needs a quick soak in boiling water for 1–2 minutes to loosen.
Heat cooking oil in a large kadai or wok over high flame until you see wisps of smoke. Add the sliced onion and stir-fry for 1–2 minutes, keeping it moving, until the edges begin to colour.

Add carrots and mushrooms and toss for 2–3 minutes over high heat until the mushrooms are wilted and fragrant. The key is to keep the flame high throughout — lower heat makes vegetables steam and turn soggy instead of getting a slight char.
Add the shredded cabbage and toss everything together for 1–2 minutes until the cabbage softens at the edges but still has a little crunch.

Add the udon noodles and pour soy sauce over the entire mixture. Use tongs or two spatulas to toss everything vigorously so the noodles separate and coat evenly. Stir-fry for 2 more minutes on high heat.
Remove from heat, drizzle sesame oil over the top and grind over plenty of black pepper. Toss once more, then pile into bowls and scatter spring onion and sesame seeds on top. Eat immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: noodles → thick Chinese hakka noodles (boiled) or even thick cooked wheat vermicelli (semiya) (closest texture widely available in India); soy-sauce → 2 tbsp coconut aminos + 1 tsp salt for a soy-free version (slightly sweeter flavour); gingelly-oil → omit or use a tiny drop of mustard oil for a pungent Indian twist — it changes the flavour significantly (toasted sesame oil is the authentic choice); mushroom → thinly sliced capsicum or broccoli florets for variety (add at the same stage)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A large kadai on maximum flame is ideal for wok-style stir-frying — pre-heat it for 2 minutes before adding oil so it is genuinely hot. Thick Chinese hakka noodles from the local store are the best substitute for udon. Soy sauce is widely available in India; sesame oil (gingelly oil / nallennai) is stocked in most South Indian grocery stores.
